---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
#library(stargazer)
#library(kableExtra)
#library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-data_blue_v3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, inverse

.size70[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size40[
Practice building, polishing and interpreting univariate analyses

- Script: Univariate_Analyses_FreedomHouse.R

- Data 1: FreedomHouse-2021-tidy.xlsx

- Data 2: FreedomHouse-2013-2021-tidy.xlsx
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Review submissions on Canvas

<br>

<br>

Today we practice our univariate analysis tools by examining the Freedom House data.

### In very broad strokes, what is the purpose of the measures produced by Freedom House? What are they trying to measure?

- (**SLIDE**)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/05_2-Freedom_House1.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

???

Freedom House's "Freedom in the World" project is an attempt to quantify "freedom" in countries across the world in terms of "political rights" and "civil liberties"

<br>

### According to Freedom House, what is the difference between "political rights" and "civil liberties"?

- Political Rights in three subcategories:
    1. Electoral Process (3 questions), 
    2. Political Pluralism and Participation (4), and
    3. Functioning of Government (3).
    
- Civil liberties in four subcategories:
    1. Freedom of Expression and Belief (4 questions),
    2. Associational and Organizational Rights (3), 
    3. Rule of Law (4), and 
    4. Personal Autonomy and Individual Rights (4).)

<br>

If we had chosen Freedom House as our data set we would need to spend quite a bit of time digging into these conceptual labels.

- How distinct are they? 

- Are these valid definitions? Are they reliable?

### Did any of the individual questions stand out to you as being a problem in terms of their validity or reliability?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/05_2-Freedom_House2.png')
background-size: 90%
background-class: center
class: middle

???

The process in oversimplified terms: 

- Each country is evaluated by experts across the 25 questions (PR = 10, CL = 15)

- Each question scored between 0 (the smallest degree of freedom) and 4 (the greatest degree of freedom)

- The results are summed together to give separate scores for PR (0 to 40) and CL (0 to 60)

- These two numbers are then combined using this table to produce a categorization of the country as being "free", "partly free" or "not free"

### Make sense?

<br>

An incredibly ambitious undertaking with LOTS of sources for uncertainty!

- Our focus today is on analyzing the measured data, but I never want you to forget that "meaning" is more a function of research design as it is statistics.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size70[.content-box-blue[**Our Job for Today**]]]

<br>

.size65[
1. What is the current state of "freedom" in the world?

2. How is "freedom" trending across time in the world?
]

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# Load readxl package
library(readxl)

# Input data as new object
data_2021 <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/Freedom_House/FreedomHouse-2021-tidy.xlsx")
```

???

On Wednesday we discussed and tested out different ways to add context to our univariate tools that would deepen our analyses.

### What three "contexts" did we add?
- (The use of benchmarks, maps and across time data.)

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's follow that as an outline for our work today.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.content-box-blue[.size60[**What is the current state of "freedom" in the world?**]]

.size55[
**Option 1: Check the Benchmarks**

Compare the distribution of `status` to the distribution of the `total` score in 2021
]

???

### What type of variable is `status`?

### - And what tools do we use when we have a categorical variable?

<br>

### What type of variable is `total`?

### - And what tools do we use when we have a numerical variable?

<br>

Ok, get to it!

- **SLIDE**: My Version
- **SLIDE**: The code




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=1, out.width='100%', fig.width=4}
# Bar plot
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "seagreen") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(20, 40, 60, 80), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free"))
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=1, out.width='100%', fig.width=4}
# Histogram
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = total)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, color = "white", fill = "seagreen") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "Total Freedom House Score (2021)", y = "Country Count")
```
]

???

### What is our takeaway from these two plots? What is the state of "freedom" in 2021?

### - How helpful is `status` as a benchmark for "good" and "bad" values?
- (Debatabale, no?)

<br>

### What is a good title for each of these plots? Something that directs the viewer to an important conclusion?






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)

# Load the data
data_2021 <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/Freedom_House/FreedomHouse-2021-tidy.xlsx")

# Bar plot
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "seagreen") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(20, 40, 60, 80), color = "white") + #<<
  theme_bw() + #<<
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free"))

# Histogram
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = total)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, color = "white", fill = "seagreen") +
  theme_bw() + #<<
  labs(x = "Total Freedom House Score (2021)", y = "Country Count")
```
]

???

New code bits

- ggplot has built in themes for changing the general look of your plot
    - theme_bw is good for printing to black and white
    
- geom_hline adds horizontal lines to your plot
    - I like a bar plot with the bars divided into sections, it helps me compare sizes more easily
    
<br>

### What's another piece of context we could add to go deeper in our analyses of FH scores?

- (**SLIDE**: Maps and time!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.content-box-blue[.size70[**What is the current state of "freedom" in the world?**]]

.size55[
**Context Option 2: Check the Map**

Build a world map for `total` in 2021
]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

```{r, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='hide'}
# Load the package
library(rworldmap)

# Merge the data
data_2021a <- joinCountryData2Map(dF = data_2021, joinCode = "NAME", nameJoinColumn = "country")
```

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.6, out.width='100%', fig.width=12}
# Output the map
mapCountryData(data_2021a, nameColumnToPlot = "total", colourPalette = "topo", mapTitle = "Freedom House: Total Scores (2021)")
```

???

### What is our takeaway from this map?

<br>

### What is a good title for this plot? Something that directs the viewer to an important conclusion?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code120[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Install the rworldmap package (only once per computer)
install.packages("rworldmap")

# Load the package
library(rworldmap)

# Merge your data onto the world map
data_2021a <- joinCountryData2Map(dF = data_2021, 
                                  joinCode = "NAME", 
                                  nameJoinColumn = "country")

# Output the map
mapCountryData(data_2021a, 
               nameColumnToPlot = "total", 
               colourPalette = "topo", 
               mapTitle = "Freedom House: Total Scores (2021)")
```
]

???

To help keep the code readable I'm showing you multiple lines within each function.

- This code will work, but I suggest you keep your code on a single line until you're more comfortable diagnosing errors.

<br>

**SLIDE**: Let's look at one more way to answer the "current state of the world question."

    
    
    
---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size40[**What is the current state of "freedom" in the world?**]]]

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=1, out.width='100%', fig.width=4}
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = pr)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, color = "white", fill = "lightsteelblue3") +
  theme_bw() + #<<
  labs(x = "Political Rights Scores (2021)", y = "Country Count")
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=1, out.width='100%', fig.width=4}
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = cl)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, color = "white", fill = "darkseagreen3") +
  theme_bw() + #<<
  labs(x = "Civil Liberties Scores (2021)", y = "Country Count")
```
]

???

As you know, the Freedom House total score is made up of two component pieces.
- Political rights and civil liberties

### What do we learn from visualizing these two distributions as histograms?
- *force this discussion!*

<br>

**SLIDE**: Ok, let's jump to our second question
    


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size70[**How is "freedom" trending across time in the world?**]]]

.size45[.center[**Context Option 3: Use the filter**]
1. Load the dataset: FreedomHouse-2013-2021-tidy.xlsx

2. Create a subset of the data for 2013 (filter)

3. Make a bar plot of `status` for 2013 and 2021
]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=1, out.width='100%', fig.width=4}
# Input data as new object
data_all <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/Freedom_House/FreedomHouse-2013-2021-tidy.xlsx")

# Create a subset of the data
data_2013 <- filter(data_all, edition == 2013)

# Bar plot 2013
ggplot(data_2013, aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "seagreen") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count", title = "2013 Edition") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free")) +
  ylim(0, 100) #<<
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=1, out.width='100%', fig.width=4}
# Bar plot 2021
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "seagreen") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count", title = "2021 Edition") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free")) +
  ylim(0, 100) #<<
```
]

???

### What do we learn from visualizing these two distributions as bar plots?
- *force this discussion!*

- Slight decrease in "free" states, but this really isn't the ideal way to make this comparison...

<br>

**SLIDE**: Code on the next slide, but I also want to show you a better way to make this comparison.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code100[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Input data as new object
data_all <- read_excel("../../Course_History/2023-Spring/Data_in_Class-SP23/Freedom_House/FreedomHouse-2013-2021-tidy.xlsx")

# Create a subset of the data
data_2013 <- filter(data_all, edition == 2013)

# Bar plot 2013
ggplot(data_2013, aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "seagreen") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count", title = "2013 Edition") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free")) +
  ylim(0, 100)

# Bar plot 2021
ggplot(data_2021, aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "seagreen") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(25, 50, 75), color = "white") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count", title = "2021 Edition") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free")) +
  ylim(0, 100)
```
]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.retina=3, fig.asp=.75, out.width='80%', fig.width=7}
data_all |>
  mutate(
    status_f = factor(status, levels = c("F", "PF", "NF"))
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = edition, fill = status_f)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_brewer(type = "div", palette = 5, direction = -1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  labs(x = "", y = "Proportion of Countries") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = 2013:2021) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  annotate("text", x = 2013, y = .125, label = "NF") +
  annotate("text", x = 2013, y = .41, label = "PF") +
  annotate("text", x = 2013, y = .8, label = "F")
```

???

We'll get to this code in a few weeks, but this is an example of a bivariate analysis using a stacked bar plot converted into proportions of the total.

### Questions on how to read this plot?

<br>

### What do we learn from this about the trend of freedom across time?

<br>

**SLIDE**: Last approach from Wednesday, animation!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.content-box-blue[.size70[**How is "freedom" trending across time in the world?**]]]

.size45[.center[**Context Option 3: Animate!**]
1. Use the dataset: FreedomHouse-2013-2021-tidy.xlsx

2. Copy our animation code from Wednesday and track FH `status` across time
]

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE, fig.retina=3, fig.asp=0.618,  fig.align='center', out.width='90%', fig.width=7, cache=TRUE}
# Load the package
library(gganimate)

# Histogram across time
data_all |>
  ggplot(aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "cadetblue2") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count", title = "{as.integer(frame_time)}") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free")) +
  transition_time(edition)
```

???



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.code120[
```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# Load the package
library(gganimate)

# Histogram across time
data_all |>
  ggplot(aes(x = status)) +
  geom_bar(width = .5, fill = "cadetblue2") +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Country Count", 
       title = "{as.integer(frame_time)}") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("NF", "PF", "F"), 
                   labels = c("Not Free", "Partly Free", "Free")) +
  transition_time(edition)
```
]

???

### What is our takeaway from this animation? How is "freedom" trending over time?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.center[.size70[.content-box-blue[**Our Job for Today**]]]

<br>

.size65[
1. What is the current state of "freedom" in the world?

2. How is "freedom" trending across time in the world?
]

???

### Ok, how did we do today?

<br>

### Ready to go on the first report next week?
